texts.label: text
texts.orig: Uncontrolled release of CO2 from industrial sources represents an increasing challenge to solve for scientists worldwide, considering the threat to a changing climate. One approach may be to separate out the CO2 from the flue gas (for sequestration or other usage) by using membranes. Hollow fiber membrane geometry represents an optimum solution due to the high ratio of membrane area per volume [1]. CO2 removal from gases containing water vapour (flue gas, natural gas) may be challenging due to the potential swelling of the membrane materials caused by the water. texts.orig: Compositehollowfibers membraneswerepreparedbycoatingpoly(phenyleneoxide)(PPO)andpolysulfone (PSf) hollow fibers with high molecular polyvinylamine (PVAm). Two procedures of coating hollow fibers outside and respective inside were investigated with respect to intrinsic PVAm solution properties and hollow fibers geometry and material.
texts.text: Compositehollowfibers membraneswerepreparedbycoatingpoly(phenyleneoxide)(PPO)andpolysulfone (PSf) hollow fibers with high molecular polyvinylamine (PVAm). Two procedures of coating hollow fibers outside and respective inside were investigated with respect to intrinsic PVAm solution properties and hollow fibers geometry and material.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The influence of operating mode (sweep or vacuum) on the performances of membranes was investigated. Vacuum operating mode gave better results than using sweep because part of the sweep gas permeated into feed and induced an extra resistance to the most permeable gas the CO2. The composite PVAm/PSf HF membranes having a 0.7-1.5 𝛍 mPVAmselective layer, showed CO2/N2 selectivity between 100 and 230. The selectivity was attributed to the CO2 facilitated transport imposed by PVAm selective layer. The CO2 permeance changed from 0.006 to 0.022m 3 (STP)/(m 2 bar h) in direct correlation with CO2 permeance and separation mechanism of the individual porous supports used for membrane fabrication. The multilayer PVAm/PPO membrane using as support PPO hollow fibers with a 40nm PPO dense skin layer, surprisingly presented an increase in selectivity with the increase in CO2 partial pressure. This trend was opposite to the facilitated transport characteristic behaviour of PVAm/porous PSf. This indicated that PVAm/PPO membrane represents a new membrane, with new properties and a hybrid mechanism, extremely stable at high pressure ratios. The CO2/N2 selectivity ranged between 20 and 500 and the CO2 permeance from 0.11 to 2.3 m 3 (STP)/(m 2 barh) depending on the operating conditions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For both PVAm/PSf and PVAm/PPO membranes, the CO2 permeance was similar with the CO2 permeance of uncoated hollow fiber supports, confirming that the CO2 diffusion rate limiting step resides in the properties of the relatively thick support, not at the level of 1.2 𝛍 m thin and water swollen PVAm selective layer. A dynamic transfer of the CO2 diffusion rate limiting step between PVAm top layer and PPO support was observed by changing the feed relative humidity (RH%). The CO2 diffusion rate was controlled by the PPO support when using humid feed. At low feed humidity the 1.2 𝛍 mPVAmtoplayer becomes the CO2 diffusion rate limiting step.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The papers reporting on different FSC membranes [6-9,11-19] are focusing almost exclusively on the material properties of the selective layer, on the importance of water inside of the selective film for the CO2 facilitation transport, on crosslinking procedures to enhance the mechanical stability and on the presence or absence of facilitation effect. None or very few papers correlate the preparation of the composite FSC membranes with their separation performances and mechanical stability under different operation modes (sweep or vacuum) [20]. The review by Koros and Mahajan [5] also points out an important issue regarding the applicability of FSC membranes in industry: they predict a relative low stability by comparison to the glassy, dense solution-diffusion based membranes. The point of the authors is sustained by the characteristics of the FSC membranes reported in literature: selective layer with a thickness from 20 to 500 𝛍 m, thicker even than the support used as backing, maximum operating feed pressure of 5 bar and driving force created by using sweep on permeate side [8,9,13,17-19]. The large thickness of the selective layer, low pressure ratio over membranesandsweepoperatingmodeallowedpreparationoftheseFSC membranes on micro-porous supports with 0.1-0.4 𝛍 m pore size without apparently affecting the membrane performances. Only one article was found which presented a FSC membrane having selective layer of 0.3 𝛍 m, operated with vacuum and a pressure ratio of 100. This was possible by using a less porous mechanically strong PSf support (6000 MWCO) with a gutter layer of high Mw chitosan (500,000 Mw) [20].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For industrial applications, the choice of operating mode (sweep or vacuum) is mainly related to the cost in terms of energy requirements and to the complexity of the process design. The sweep as a mean of increasing the driving force has the advantage of not requiring a strong mechanical membrane. The choice of sweep is, however, rather limited to steam or cheap, easily condensable gases. This is due to the subsequent necessity of a second CO2 separation from the sweep. On the other hand, using moderate vacuum provides a pure product, consumes less energy and represents a more practical option than sweep [21].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From the engineering point of view it is extremely important to understand the implications of operating conditions in correlation with the separation properties of a FSC membrane. For solution-diffusion membranes Baker [22], Pan and Habgood [23] and Geankoplis [24] point out the importance of the partial pressure ratio over membrane ( P feed / P perm) for a desired product purity in correlation with membrane selectivity. A minimum selectivity will always be needed in order to achieve a desired purity, but for a higher selectivity, the purity of the product (CO2 in our case) is controlled mainly by the P feed / P perm. These correlations can easily be documented by theoretical studies/simulations and can be achieved by innovative system design in combination with using sweep gas or vacuum on permeate side-several papers are discussingthis[25,26]. However,thesimulationsareextrapolatingthe performance of a membrane measured under certain conditions to a variety of other conditions not tested in the lab: different sweep flow rates, higher pressure ratios, thinner membranes (higher permeance), etc. Selectivity values for CO2 /N2 between 100 and 1800 are reported for FSC membranes [8,9,13,17-19]. When the mini- mumselectivity for the system has been achieved (as documented by simulations), the permeate CO2 purity depends mostly on the membrane's ability to sustain a high pressure ratios. Consequently, focusing on preparing a pressure resistant (robust) and high CO2 permeance membrane seems to be a more reasonable approach than focusing on further increase in selectivity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The intuitive way of making high permeance membranes is by reducing the thickness of the membrane selective layer to the range of 0.1-2 𝛍 mas reported in literature [22,25]. The investigation of the supports which are used for membranes having a water swollen selective layer (gel like structure) less than 2 𝛍 m, becomes extremely important since no realistic extrapolation of separation performance and mechanical stability can be made from a thick selective layer (20-500 𝛍 m)tothat of a thin layer. All these aspects are discussed in the current paper.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Another important issue which was not addressed for the reported FSC membranes was the investigation of the limiting step for CO2 diffusion rate. For composite asymmetric membranes the overall mass transfer resistance can be treated in the terms of a resistance in series. For a dry and dense selective top layer on top of a porous support, most of the mass transfer resistance is induced by the dense selective layer [27]. The water in swollen FSC selective layer increases the CO2 diffusivity towards values between a dry, dense membrane and a liquid membrane. For a 1 𝛍 m water swollen selective layer or less, the CO2 rate limiting step seems to transfer from the selective layer to the much thicker support. Based on documented data, this is all discussed in Section 2 of the paper.

texts.orig: The wetting of the porous support represents, according to our judgement, another factor, influencing the transport through the composite FSC membranes. From investigations done in relation to membranecontactors for gas absorption, wetting of the membrane material represents a well-known effect where the wetted support represents the rate-controlling step for CO2 diffusion [28,29].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A comprehensive review on the effect of operating conditions [30] identifies hollow fibers (HF) with bore feed operated in counter current as the most favourable configuration for enrichment ratio and recovery of fast gas. Bore feed with counter current flow pattern gives the best results because the pressure differentials across the fiber wall (driving forces) for tube feed is greater than that for shell feed. However, only few studies are describing a bore side HF coating [20,31]. One of the main advantages of coating inside HF is represented by the protection of the selective layer preventing any stickiness between HF and possible damage due to handling. The position of the coating layer on the bore side or shell side of HF depends on the characteristics of the HF: inner diameter (ID), outer diameter (OD), surface porosity, length of HF. The position of selective layer must be correlated also with the process parameters such as gas flow rates and pressure applied that will impose the direction of the feed flow and eventually of the sweep flow: shell feed (out-in) or bore feed (in-out). Shell feed is preferred for high feed pressure applications such as CO2 capture from natural gas and bore feed for low pressure applications such as nitrogen separation from air [32]. In the current study, both inside and outside FSC-membrane coating of the hollow fibers were investigated.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The current study provides an approach for preparing hollow fiber multilayer composite membranes having a thin, swollen PVAmselective layer (1 𝛍 m)andinvestigations of the limiting step for CO2 diffusion rate. The study investigates the implications of the thin, water swollen selective layer coated on different supports for membrane separation performance, operating mode and mechanical stability. The effect of operating mode (sweep and vacuum) was correlated with the feed pressure ratio, with the CO2 partial pressure, membrane selectivity and permeance in an attempt to identify experimentally the optimum operating conditions for the obtained membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the membrane selective layer preparation, polyvinylamine (PVAm) with Mw 340,000 was kindly provided by BASF AG Germany. As a porous support poly(phenylene oxide) (PPO) hollow fibers kindly provided by Parker, polysulfone hollow fibers from Blue Cross China (PSF C) and home made (PSF HM) were used. Solvents used were methanol, formamide, ethylene glycol and distilled water. All the materials were used without further purification with the exception of PVAm 340,000 Mw from BASF. The PVAm polymer was purified in successive steps: re-precipitation in acetone and ethanol mixtures, washing, filtration, drying until constant weight, re-dissolution in distilled water. This procedure was repeated three times and the resulted polymer solution was hydrolysed by acidic hydrolyse [13] and re-precipitated in acetone. For permeation experiments, all the pure gases and gas mixtures with certified composition were provided by AGA Norway.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For outside coating , the membranes were prepared by dip coating technique: 50 cm long hollow fibers were immersed vertically into the coating 2% PVAm aqueous solution for 1min and were lifted out at 0.4 m/s constant speed followed by drying at 45 ◦ C for 75 min. The procedure was repeated (three coatings) until a 1-1.5 𝛍 mdefectfree film of PVAm was formed on the surface of the porous support. The resulted hollow fibers were thermally treated (annealed) at 90 ◦ C for 1 h in the following day. Several modules in glass and stainless steel were prepared for gas permeation experiments. The number of hollow fiber membranes was chosen such that the area of the selective layer coated on the membranes was 15-30cm 2 in total. Three to seven hollow fiber membranes were installed in each module, due to the difference in inner and outer diameter of the support membranes. The length of the membranes exposed to gas was 0.35m in the glass modules and 0.28m in the stainless steel module used in the experiment. Glass modules were chosen to make it possible to observe the fibers during fabrication andpermeationexperiments(insidecoating)andthestainlesssteel for high vacuum and high pressure measurements. Araldite 2012 was used for gluing the ends of the hollow fibers inside modules keeping the HF lumen open in both sides.

The resulted hollow fibers were thermally treated (annealed) at 90 ◦ C for 1 h in the following day. Several modules in glass and stainless steel were prepared for gas permeation experiments. The number of hollow fiber membranes was chosen such that the area of the selective layer coated on the membranes was 15-30cm 2 in total. Three to seven hollow fiber membranes were installed in each module, due to the difference in inner and outer diameter of the support membranes. The length of the membranes exposed to gas was 0.35m in the glass modules and 0.28m in the stainless steel module used in the experiment. Glass modules were chosen to make it possible to observe the fibers during fabrication andpermeationexperiments(insidecoating)andthestainlesssteel for high vacuum and high pressure measurements. Araldite 2012 was used for gluing the ends of the hollow fibers inside modules keeping the HF lumen open in both sides.

texts.orig: Pure gases and premixed gas with a molar composition of 10%, 20% and 50% CO2 in N2 were used as feed and methane was used as a sweep gas due to chromatographic analysis and precise determination of all components of permeate plus sweep. In real operation the use of these sweep gases are of course not an option. Both feed and sweep gases were humidified by passage through two water saturators (marked as vessels 1 in Fig. 3). The RH% was controlled by two bypass valves (V4 and V14) controlling the ratio of dry/wet gas. The compositions of the permeate and feed were analysed continuously by a micro-GC (gas chromatograph) Agilent 3000 equipped with two thermal conductivity detectors (TCDs) and two columns, Molecularsieve and Plot Q. A liquid separator was installed before GC sampling valve in order to prevent the moisture penetration inside GC.

texts.orig: The membrane selectivity ˛ of CO2 over N2 (or CH4) was calculated using the permeance ratio of the two gases when permeating together in a mixture without excluding the reciprocal coupling effect between gases (Eq. (3)). The ideal selectivity was calculated from the ratio of the pure, single gas permeances implying no interaction and no competitive transport between gases. A cer- tain ambiguity exist in literature about expressing the membranes selectivity of a gas mixture as concentration selectivity ˛ * or separation factor ˇ which represents a limit case of the membrane selectivity ( ˛ ), when the value of pressure ratio P feed / P perm is higher or equal to the value of selectivity [22]. Concentration selectivity depends strongly on the process conditions [34-36] and is more rightfully called ' process selectivity ' (Eq. (4)).

texts.orig: The high viscosity of 2% water PVAm solution turned out to be detrimental for inside coating for PSf C and PPO hollow fibers due to the small bore diameter of 0.3-0.34 mm. The high frictional forces built up the liquid pressure inside the hollow fibers bore so that the PSf C and PPO HF burst during the coating procedure. The only successful inside coating was obtained for the 0.6 mm bore PSf HM hollow fibers. From the SEM pictures the increase in thickness of the coating layer followed the same trend as in the case of outside coating (by dip coating). Apparently, the viscosity of the PVAm solution, the quality of the hollow fiber support surface controlled also the thickness of the inside coating layer. The coating outside was successful for the PSf C and PPO due to a dense outer layer (see Fig. 6a and c) and unsuccessful for PSf HM due to the presence of macrodefectsonhollowfiberssurface. The results of coating can be seen in Fig. 6. The thickness of the obtained layer was in correlation with the predicted thickness from the preliminary coating study: 1-1.5 𝛍 mfor the 3 × coated outside and 0.7 𝛍 mfor the 2 × coated inside.

texts.orig: The wall thickness of the three HF supports differs only by a factor of two but the CO2 permeance ranged from 0.0007 to 1.8 m 3 (STP)/(m 2 barh). A clear difference can be seen between PPO HF and the two types of PSf hollow fibers, due to the different separation mechanism. The 40nm dense top layer of PPO imposes a solution-diffusion transport for CO2 and the open porous structure of the two PSf imposed a Knudsen diffusion mechanism. The two different separation mechanisms of PPO and PSf become obvious from CO2/N2 ideal selectivity: PPO presented a CO2/N2 constant selectivity of 22 and both PSf HM and PSf C had sub-unitary CO2/N2 selectivity in correlation with the Knudsen diffusion [1]. PSf C presented lower permeance than PSf HM due to a relatively dense and thick outer layer (4-5 𝛍 m) (see Fig. 6a). Despite the porous substructure of both PSf hollow fibers, the low CO2 permeance may be explained by a limited number of open pores at the hollow fibers surface.

texts.orig: Uncoated, dry PSf C has a maximum CO2 permeance of 0.001m 3 (STP)/(m 2 barh) (Fig. 7) similar with the CO2 permeance of PVAm/PSf C composite membrane and the CO2/N2 ideal selectivity of 0.5 was obtained using single CO2 and N2 gas permeation at 3 bar feed pressure and room temperature. The increase in permeance, as can be seen in Table 3 is most likely caused by a water film formation in the pores of the PSf C support that provided a transition from the Knudsen diffusion towards solution-diffusion through the water film formed in pores. Some changes in the pore structure due to swelling of the PSf may also explain this behaviour. The CO2 permeance decreased using 0.1bar vacuum because some of the fibers collapsed during experiment, hence less permeation area. This fact was checked by removing the HF module from setup, feeding CH4 on the bore side (2-5 bar pressure) and observing the gas flow through individual hollow fibers on the other end of the module by using soap solution. The CO2/N2 selectivity appears to increase slightly with feed pressure, but not a definitive and clear conclusion could be made about the trend. Due to the low CO2 permeance in correlation with the CO2 permeance of the uncoated support, the PSf C hollow fibers were not investigated further.

texts.orig: efficiency of the sweep may be affected (channelling, dead volume, etc.). The influence of sweep flow rate on permeance and selectivity was investigated and the results are shown in Fig. 8. As can be seen from Fig. 8a, the CO2 permeance was low at low values of sweep rates indicating the effect of the large permeate side volume. Increasing the sweep flow rate, the CO2 permeance of the PVAm/PSf HM approaches the permeance of the uncoated PSf HM support. Fig. 8b shows that also CO2 /N2 selectivity of PVAm/PSf HM increases with sweep flow rate. Both CO2 permeance and CO2/N2 selectivity increased with the sweep flow rate due to the increased driving force. The membrane selectivity (permeance ratio) is equal with the process selectivity (molar ratio) for all the sweep flow rate range due to the relatively low CO2 permeance. The similarity between the CO2 permeance for uncoated PSf HM and composite PVAm/PSf HM at 100% RH humidity presented in Fig. 9a confirms the data obtained for coated outside PVAm/PSf C: that CO2 diffusion rate limiting step resides on the support and mass transfer resistance on the thin swollen PVAm top layer is negligible. The fact that both CO2 permeance and CO2/N2 selectivity for PVAm/PSf HM decreases by increasing the feed pressure (CO2 partial pressure) due to CO2 carrier saturation, confirms the facilitation effect of the PVAm FSC selective layer.

texts.orig: Polyphenylene oxide (PPO) represents one of the most investigated polymeric materials for CO2 separation from different gas mixtures due to the high CO2 permeance and relative good CO2/N2 selectivity of 20. A multitude of studies reported different modifications of PPO in order to increase the separation performances: benzoylation [39] sulfonation [40], modification with bulky groups such as hyperbranched polyesters (boltorn) or fullerene that increased the permeance preserving a constant selectivity [41,42], metals Na, Mg [43] where a maximum selectivity of ˛ CO2 /CH 4 = 40-54 but a low 0.002m 3 (STP)/(m 2 bar h) permeance was obtained.

Consequently, the sweep inefficiently decreases the CO2 partial pressure on the permeate, dilutes/decreases the CO2 partial pressure on the feed/retentate side, hence lower driving force for CO2 transport across membrane.

texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two procedures of coating hollow fibers on shell side and respective bore side were developed in correlation with the characteristics of PSf and PPO hollow fibers and intrinsic PVAm properties. The success of coating procedures was entirely dependent on the solution viscosity, the geometry of HF and on the quality of the HF surface.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The effect of sweep and vacuum for membrane performances was investigated under different P feed / P perm. This analysis provided new information, which was not obvious using only the permeance and selectivity of membranes for investigation. High CO2 permeance for PVAm/PPO membranes implies simultaneously high permeance for sweep and part of the sweep gas diffused into the feed inducing an extra resistance to the CO2 permeance. As a consequence, for high permeable membranes, moderate vacuum represents a more practical choice for membrane separation performances, process cost and product purity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For both PVAm/PSf and PVAm/PPO operated with humidified feed gas, the CO2/N2 selectivity is provided by the CO2 facilitated transport imposed by the PVAm selective layer. Surprisingly, the CO2 permeance of humidified composite membranes was similar with the CO2 permeance of the wet, uncoated HF used for membrane fabrication. In consequence, the CO2 diffusion rate limiting step resides in the properties of the relatively thick support, not at the level of 1.2 𝛍 m thin and water swollen PVAm selective layer. A dynamic transfer of CO2 diffusion rate limiting step between PVAm top layer and PPO support was observed by changing the feed relative humidity. For all the FSC composite membranes, the CO2 diffusion rate is controlled by the support at high humidity levels. At low humidity of the feed, the 1.2 𝛍 mPVAmdry top layer becomes the CO2 diffusion rate limiting step The CO2 permeance of the composite membrane becomes much smaller than the CO2 permeance of the wet uncoated porous support.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The PVAm/PPO membranes prepared using hollow fibers with a 40nm dense skin layer, surprisingly presented an increase in CO2/N2 selectivity with the increase in CO2 partial pressure. This trend is opposite to the characteristic behaviour of facilitated transport presented by PVAm/PSf and most of the FSC reported in literature. This is believed to indicate that PVAm/PPO membrane represents a new membrane with a hybrid transport mechanism, mechanically stable at up to a pressure ratio of 3900. The high mechanical stability of 1.2 𝛍 m water swollen PVAm is due to the correlation of the non-porous PPO surface with the high Mw of PVAm (340,000) in absence of any additional crosslinker. Obviously, the choice and investigation of the support material become essential in providing high CO2 permeance and mechanical resistance for a composite FSC membrane operated with humid feed. The CO2/N2 selectivity ranged between 20 and 500 and the CO2 permeance from 0.11 to 2.3m 3 (STP)/(m 2 barh) under different operating conditions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The increase in CO2/N2 selectivity with feed pressure indicates that PVAm/PPO membranes can be tailored also for CO2 removal at high pressure in applications such as natural gas sweetening.
